summary: Chapel Hill's Community Center offers an indoor climbing wall with over 40 routes and a bouldering section for all skill levels, from beginners to experts. The facility is open to individuals six years and older and provides attendants, equipment rental, and requires belay testing for safety. Climbers under 18 need a signed release form.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: The Chapel Hill Online Permit Center automatically sends email notifications to applicants for each step in the permit process, including inspections. While there's no option to turn off these notifications currently, applicants can check permit status via their user dashboard and contact the Town for assistance. Fees for building permits are due after administrative review, and trade-only permits are due upon issuance.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Chapel Hill Community Design Commission, which meets virtually, advises the Town Council on aesthetics, character, and function to guide community growth. It provides advice and advocacy, implements Council policies, and reviews proposed development in key areas of the community.

Chapel Hill outlines age requirements for various activities, including limits on the number of children in different age groups (birth to 13 years) and volunteer age requirements. Volunteers aged 14-15 require three references, and those 16 and older require a background check.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill's legislative procedures dictate voting requirements for ordinances. General ordinances require a two-thirds vote for immediate enactment or a simple majority at a second reading. Budget ordinances and amendments to development regulations can be adopted by a simple majority. Franchise ordinances need approval by a five-vote majority at two regular meetings. Specific rules apply to tie votes and abstentions by Council members.

Alicia Arnold has been appointed as the new Director of Housing & Community Development for Chapel Hill. She will oversee initiatives to expand affordable housing access, strengthen services for unhoused community members, and manage the town's affordable housing plan, investment strategy, public housing communities, and community development funding programs, including over $5 million in federal and local funding.

Carrboro's Watershed Residential Assistance Program (WRAP) offers free stormwater site assessments to single-family homeowners to improve water management on their properties. The program aims to reduce runoff, slow erosion, improve water quality, protect streams, and enhance community resilience to stormwater challenges. Recommendations include nature-based practices, and homeowners may qualify for technical assistance and cost-sharing for eligible projects.

The Carrboro Music Festival is an annual event celebrating local musicians from the Carrboro, Chapel Hill, and Orange County areas, as well as surrounding counties. Performers apply to participate and receive a $250 honorarium if selected. The festival, scheduled for September 27, 2026, provides sound systems at many venues, and musicians are encouraged to sell merchandise.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Latasha 'Tasha' Pegues has been selected as the new Human Resources Director for the Town of Carrboro, starting June 29, 2026. With over 23 years of experience in public sector HR, Pegues will focus on strengthening the workforce, advancing a diverse and supportive workplace culture, investing in employee development, and supporting the Town's mission and strategic priorities.

Carrboro addresses various parking and towing regulations. Public parking lots generally do not allow overnight parking. The Town is implementing a wayfinding program for public parking. Private lots, like Carr Mill Mall, are for specific uses and can tow non-compliant vehicles, provided they adhere to State statutes and Town ordinances regarding signage, notification to Police, and payment acceptance. The Town Police Department investigates complaints about improper towing.

impacted_stakeholders: Black, Indigenous, and People of Color
summary: The Town of Carrboro is a national leader in climate action, aiming for an 80% reduction in community-wide and municipal greenhouse gas emissions by 2030, compared to a 2010 baseline. Key objectives include increasing energy efficiency in Town buildings, electrifying the Town fleet, adding renewable energy to facilities, increasing access to renewable energy and efficiency programs for residents and businesses, encouraging environmentally friendly transportation, reducing waste, and developing a system-wide approach to build resilience to climate change impacts, particularly for low-income and historically marginalized neighborhoods.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Carrboro conducts biennial community surveys to assess resident satisfaction with the quality of life, services, and overall experience in the town. Results from recent surveys (e.g., 2026, 2024) consistently show high resident satisfaction, with nearly all (98%) residents rating Carrboro as 'excellent' or 'good' as a place to live, raise children, and feel welcome. Carrboro often outperforms regional and national benchmarks in these surveys.

Construction for 'The 203 Project,' a new library and civic building with an associated parking deck in Carrboro, is ongoing. The project involves significant concrete pours for the structure and parking deck, interior construction, and exterior finishing like brick veneer. Temporary road closures and traffic impacts are expected during certain phases of the work, which is projected to be completed next summer.

The Zoning Division is responsible for assisting the Town Council and advisory boards, processing permits, reviewing development proposals for compliance with the Land Use Ordinance, and providing public information. Recent initiatives include public hearings and approval of Land Use Ordinance Text Amendments to modify standards related to height, residential density, and non-residential uses in the R-2 conditional district, specifically for a five-story multi-family residential project at 1307 West Main Street.

The Carrboro Police Department provides 24/7 law enforcement and community services, employing 43 people, including 39 sworn officers. They handle emergency and non-emergency situations, dispatching officers through Orange County Communications (911 call center). The department also offers an email alert system for criminal activity and participates in OCAlerts to notify residents about major incidents.

The Town of Carrboro recently completed the repaving of E. Poplar Street as part of its annual resurfacing project. The improvements include new striping with sharrow symbols, indicating shared use for cars and bicycles, and a wider buffered shoulder to enhance safety for pedestrians. The design aims to balance the needs of all road users, emergency services, and public works operations. The project was funded by the NC Department of Transportation.

The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department offers a comprehensive Community Risk Reduction program to promote greater fire safety. Services include fire truck visits for community events, child car seat installation checks, courtesy home inspections, fire extinguisher classes for groups, smoke and/or carbon monoxide alarm installation assistance, public education events, and station tours.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
impacted_stakeholders: Carrboro residents with limited English proficiency
summary: The Town of Carrboro adopted a Language Access Plan in April 2024 to ensure timely access to translation and interpretation services for its residents. The plan requires the Town to make best efforts to provide these services, translate its most commonly distributed documents into six designated townwide languages (Spanish, Chinese, Korean, Karen, Burmese, and Arabic, with an initial focus on Spanish), and post signage about the availability of free interpretation services. Residents can file complaints if language services are not received.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town of Carrboro actively uses official social media channels, including Facebook, Instagram, Bluesky, Nextdoor, YouTube, and LinkedIn, to disseminate news, events, and updates to the community. These platforms are used by the Town of Carrboro, its Police and Fire-Rescue Departments, and for promoting various cultural and local events.

Carrboro promotes local arts and culture through initiatives such as the '2nd Friday Art Walk' and various art exhibitions. These events provide opportunities for residents to engage with public art, including murals, and enjoy the town's vibrant cultural scene.

Construction of the Drakeford Library Complex at 203 S. Greensboro Street, a $42 million public facility in Carrboro. It will house the Orange County Southern Branch Library, Carrboro Recreation, Parks and Cultural Resources, Orange County Skills Development Center, WCOM Radio, a teen center, and multipurpose spaces. The LEED Gold equivalent building will feature a 171-space parking deck (including EV charging) and 70 bike spots, along with stormwater control measures. The project, a joint effort with Orange County, is expected to open in February 2025.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Establishment and ongoing work of the Racial Equity Commission (REC) in Carrboro, formed in 2017 with the Carrboro Truth Plaque Community Task Force (merged Dec 2024). The REC educates on historical racism, amplifies BIPOC voices, and influences policy to prioritize equity and justice, aiming for a community of inclusiveness. Members are appointed by the Town Council and receive race and equity training. It also involves the installation of truth plaques, acknowledging Carrboro's past.

Creation of the Safe Routes to School Implementation Committee in Carrboro, composed of ten voting members and three non-voting youth advisory members. The committee recommends transportation projects, policies, programs, and activities to increase the safety and convenience of walking and bicycling to elementary and middle schools within Town limits. It also provides guidance for implementing the Safe Routes to School Action Plan and assists with funding efforts.

Carrboro's Economic Development initiatives focus on supporting the local business community, particularly locally owned businesses. This includes offering two revolving loan programs to promote job creation, business retention, and energy efficiency. The department also staffs the Carrboro Tourism Development Authority and celebrates Small Business Week to highlight the contributions of local entrepreneurs.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Carrboro's policy regarding the composition and appointment of members to various advisory commissions, including the Housing and Human Services Advisory Commission and the Youth Council. This involves appointing voting and non-voting liaison members from the Town Council, Town staff, and other advisory bodies to ensure diverse representation and expertise in town governance.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Carrboro's policy for the recruitment, appointment, and operation of its various advisory boards and commissions. Citizens apply to serve, attend meetings, and are appointed by the Town Council. These volunteers play a vital role in interpreting town codes, advising elected officials, and addressing citizen appeals. The policy also includes rules of procedure, recruitment policies, and mandatory training for new members.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Recognition of the Town of Carrboro's Communications and Engagement Department for excellence in communications through the North Carolina City & County Communicators (NC3C) awards. The town received awards for photography, graphic design (bus wrap), and crisis communications (Tropical Storm Chantal response), demonstrating its commitment to keeping residents informed, engaged, and connected through timely and compassionate public information efforts.

The Climate and Environmental Advisory Commission in Carrboro includes members like Nicholas Stover, Robert Pinder, and Lauren Nyland, who serve to advise the town on climate and environmental matters. This initiative highlights the town's commitment to involving community members in environmental policy and planning.

Carrboro's Public Works Department implements a Roadway Hazard Reduction Program to respond to winter weather, reducing hazards from ice and snow on Town-maintained streets, facilities, bike paths, sidewalks, and parking lots. Streets are prioritized based on traffic volume, transit routes, essential services, and proximity to schools, with a commitment to effectively utilize town and, if needed, contractor resources.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Carrboro's annual operating budget process ensures a balanced budget is adopted by June 30 each year for the upcoming fiscal year (July 1 - June 30). The Town Manager recommends a budget, which then undergoes public hearings and work sessions with the Mayor and Town Council for review and adjustments before final approval, setting tax rates and fee schedules for Town operations.
Installation of bicycle detection systems at four signalized intersections to improve safety and reduce delays for cyclists by triggering traffic signals.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Issuance of various proclamations and resolutions throughout the year to recognize and celebrate diverse community events, awareness campaigns, and historical observances, reflecting town governance and community engagement.

impacted_stakeholders: Housing and Human Services Commission members
summary: Listing of members of the Housing and Human Services Commission within the Town Directory, indicating focus on housing and human services initiatives.
Recognition and celebration of Black History Month through local events, promoting learning about the lives, history, and contributions of Black people, and advancing racial equity in Carrboro.

impacted_stakeholders: residents interested in recreational and cultural activities
summary: Monthly events newsletter from the Recreation, Parks & Cultural Resources Department to inform residents about upcoming programs and special events, with registration details.

Celebration of Police Week and Peace Officers Memorial Day, recognizing the Carrboro Police Department's role in public safety, crime investigation, community services, ongoing training, and crisis response, including the hiring of social workers to deflect low-level offenses.

Updates on utility work by Duke Energy and concrete pours affecting traffic on Roberson Street, S. Greensboro Street, and Maple Avenue, with detours and intermittent delays expected around a construction site.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Proclamation of Transgender Day of Remembrance in Carrboro, honoring lives lost to violence against transgender people, acknowledging the rollback of rights, and urging legislative progress for safety and equality.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Mayor's Moment update discussing ongoing budget discussions, priority-setting, updates on municipal greenhouse gas emissions and decarbonization goals, recovery efforts from Tropical Storm Chantal, stormwater program, and transportation projects, emphasizing civic engagement.
Information regarding seasonal program registration, outlining three annual registration sessions, and the availability of program brochures at least two weeks prior to registration.

Carrboro provides information and resources for disaster response, including details from the American Red Cross and SBA Disaster Field Operations Centers, which serve as one-stop-shops for those affected by natural disasters, covering business recovery, disaster loans, and food safety after power outages.

The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department offers various community safety services, including child car seat inspections/installations, reflective address sign orders, smoke and carbon monoxide alarm installations/inspections, fire prevention permits, public education events, fire station tours, and visits to community block parties or birthday parties.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This initiative maintains the Town Directory for the Board of Adjustment, listing members and their roles, facilitating transparency in town governance.

The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department responds to structure fires, detailing the efforts of the department and assisting agencies to extinguish fires, assess damage, and provide support for displaced residents and injured individuals, with ongoing investigations into fire causes.

Carrboro is actively working to prevent the spread of invasive species as part of its Community Climate Action Plan. This initiative includes raising awareness, educating residents on identifying and removing invasive plants, encouraging the planting of native species, and promoting volunteer efforts for public land cleanups, highlighted by a National Invasive Species Awareness Week proclamation.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This initiative outlines the Carrboro Town Council's meeting schedule, including regular business meetings on the first and third Tuesdays, work sessions on the second Tuesdays (where public comment is not accepted and votes are not taken), and a new meeting time for 2024.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town of Carrboro launched a redesigned website (www.carrboronc.gov) to improve accessibility, functionality, and streamline access to information and services. New features include an improved calendar, links to Town Pillars (Race and Equity, Climate Action), a projects area, translation function, video features, social media content, and ADA compliance, aiming to enhance community engagement and communication.

Carrboro supports and celebrates poetry and arts through initiatives like the West End Poetry Festival, which has featured numerous acclaimed poets. The town collaborates with community partners to program cultural events, including film festivals and pride celebrations, highlighting the role of poets laureate and local artists.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
impacted_stakeholders: Climate and Environmental Advisory Commission members
summary: This initiative involves maintaining the town directory, specifically highlighting vacant seats on key commissions such as the Economic Sustainability Commission and the Climate and Environmental Advisory Commission, which are crucial for town governance and sustainability efforts.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Carrboro Town Council holds regular meetings and town hall events to engage residents on policy changes at state and federal levels, such as proposed legislation impacting flags on government properties and Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) programs. The Town Manager shares the town's preparatory actions for these higher-level policies, encouraging public participation.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: The Planning Commission assists the Council in achieving the Town’s comprehensive plan for orderly growth and development by analyzing, evaluating, and recommending responsible town policies, ordinances, and planning standards that manage land use and involve the community in long-range planning. Meetings are often virtual webinars.

Chapel Hill has approved a total of $25 million in bond funding through municipal bond referendums to support affordable housing projects, including new construction, preservation, and land acquisition. This initiative aims to align with the Town's Affordable Housing Plan and Investment Strategy, leveraging state and federal funding and local partnerships. The bonds are expected to support the development and preservation of hundreds of affordable housing units.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This topic outlines the procedures for Chapel Hill Council meetings, including the preparation and order of agendas, methods for adding and removing items, public participation guidelines, voting rules for various actions (ordinances, resolutions, motions), and specific procedures for public hearings and closed sessions. It details the roles of the Mayor, Council members, and staff in town governance.

Chapel Hill provides services for yard trimming collection, including guidelines for pile size and options for large brush piles. Residents can take one truckload of yard trimmings to Orange County Waste and Recycling Center for free. For larger piles, a Knuckleboom Truck Collection service is available for a fee. Certain materials like loose leaves, large logs, and clearing debris are not picked up by the town.

impacted_stakeholders: North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services
summary: Chapel Hill offers personalized support for small businesses through its Business Vitality Manager, assisting with growth, permitting, relocation, and market data. The town also outlines permit requirements for operating childcare facilities, distinguishing between Family Child Care Homes and Child Care Centers, and noting additional state-level applications.

Chapel Hill mandates annual inspections of Stormwater Control Measures (SCMs) by licensed North Carolina Professional Engineers or Landscape Architects with qualified stormwater management inspection and maintenance training. Property owners are responsible for these inspections, and professionals must provide proof of certification or training upon request. The town provides a self-reported list of qualified professionals.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: Chapel Hill requires construction permits for the installation or alteration of various systems, including smoke control/exhaust systems, solar photovoltaic power systems, and outdoor stationary cryogenic fluid storage systems (exceeding certain capacities). Routine maintenance performed in accordance with the NC Fire Code is explicitly stated not to require a permit.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill conducted an Employee Pay and Classification Study, resulting in a new Pay Plan with updated pay grades, salary ranges, and job titles. This initiative aims to ensure fair compensation and provide clear career progression paths for employees. It involved input from employees and supervisors, benchmarking against peer organizations, and includes plans for yearly increases and detailed job progressions.

Chapel Hill defines dimensional standards for various housing types, including accessory apartments and two-family units. These standards cover minimum lot size, maximum floor area, height limits, impervious surface ratio, occupancy limits (e.g., maximum 4 unrelated people), and parking requirements. These regulations are part of the town's zoning and land use management.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: Chapel Hill's Zoning Compliance Permit process involves different levels of review (Limited, Administrative, Final Plans) for various types of work, from food trucks to new developments. The process requires detailed site plans that demonstrate compliance with zoning regulations, including considerations for Resource Conservation Districts, floodplains, and Jordan Watershed Riparian Buffers. Resources like GIS maps and zoning lookup tools are provided to assist applicants.

This initiative involves the use and update of FEMA flood maps and risk assessments to help communities, homeowners, and other stakeholders understand and manage flood risk, including processes for map amendments and revisions.

The Chapel Hill Police Department offers services including house checks for residents out of town and a Virtual Report Unit for filing non-emergency police reports (e.g., harassment, identity theft, lost/stolen property, damage to property/vehicle) by phone, in-person, or online. Incident and arrest reports are also viewable online.

This initiative outlines the operational requirements for Short-Term Rentals (STRs) in Chapel Hill, including rules for simultaneous rentals (prohibited in dedicated STRs, permitted in primary residences if the operator is on-site), maximum overnight occupancy, designation of a local responsible party, compliance with noise and parking ordinances, special event permits, prohibition of advertising signs, payment of taxes, minimum rental age (18 for primary guest), minimum rental duration (18 hours), and limits on dedicated STR units in multi-family developments.

impacted_stakeholders: Asian American and Pacific Islander population
organizations and individuals working on affordable housing
summary: Chapel Hill Council proclaimed May 2026 as Public Works Week, Asian American and Pacific Islander Month, Affordable Housing Month, and Historic Preservation Month, and June 18, 2026, as Community Home Trust Day, recognizing contributions and encouraging participation in related initiatives. The Council also received an update on the Climate Action Plan and approved a $318,000 economic development incentive package for Blue Sky Robotics based on job creation.

Melissa McCullough, a Chapel Hill Council Member (2023-2027), has a background in environmental protection and sustainability, having worked for the US EPA and served on the Planning Commission, Environmental Stewardship Advisory Board, Orange County Climate Council, and Sierra Club, focusing on land use planning, water quality, air pollution, and community health.

Chapel Hill is establishing a 'Percent for Public Art Program' for Town-funded capital improvement projects, where artwork location will generally be on the project site. The Town Council will annually consider a Public Art Plan recommended by the Public Arts Commission.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: The Historic District Commission in Chapel Hill manages local historic districts and landmarks by enforcing the Chapel Hill Historic Districts Design Principles & Standards, which guide physical changes and determine the congruity of proposals with the districts' character. Property owners must obtain a Certificate of Appropriateness (COA) for projects, and the Commission holds quasi-judicial meetings for review.

Cedar Falls Park in Chapel Hill is undergoing improvements, including paving of the parking lot (expected July 2026) and reconstruction of tennis courts with new sustainable LED lighting, which are substantially complete and expected to open in summer 2026. The park offers athletic fields, a playground, and trails, and facilities can be rented.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill has established a Naming Committee, appointed by the Town Council, to manage the naming, renaming, or dedicating of Town facilities (buildings, parks, greenways, etc.) and residential streets. The process involves receiving public petitions, seeking ideas from community organizations, inviting public comments, and submitting recommendations to the Council. Separate policies exist for the sale of naming rights for portions of facilities and memorial markers at Peace and Justice Plaza.

Chapel Hill offers preschool aquatic programs for children aged 3-5, focusing on basic water skills, comfort in water, and developing independent aquatic locomotion. These programs cater to children with little or no prior water experience who can separate from parents, and build on fundamental skills like submerging heads and blowing bubbles.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill outlines procedures for volunteers, including background and driving history checks for those working with vulnerable populations or driving for the town. Volunteers must complete an online application and agreement.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill outlines procedures for making reservations for Council members, including travel advances requiring two weeks for processing. The town also specifies a $100 security deposit for facility rentals, with exemptions for non-profit groups hosting public-benefiting, non-commercial programs.

Chapel Hill provides a free stream determination service to classify streams (perennial, intermittent, ephemeral) and waterbodies on properties using State and Town criteria. This classification helps determine Resource Conservation District (RCD) boundaries. Property owners can request a determination, and surveyors can delineate RCDs based on the report. Field verifications are valid for 5 years, and disagreements with classifications can be appealed.

Chapel Hill outlines methods for billing impervious surface fees for developments, allowing for proportional billing based on interior floor area or shared billing where associations pay for common areas and unit owners for private property. Clear delineation of common areas is required for shared billing.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill has a policy to select the most suitable firms or individuals to provide professional architectural and engineering services to the Town. The selection is based on demonstrated competence and qualifications to meet the required scope of services, under contractual or performance agreements.

Chapel Hill requires special event permits for outdoor events on public property (parks, sidewalks, streets, parking lots) and for events on private property if they impact nearby residents or businesses, are publicly advertised, or are near a public street/parking lot.

Chapel Hill Transit operates the 'Tar Heel Express' shuttle service for fans attending UNC-Chapel Hill home football and men's basketball games, and other major campus events. The service provides continuous transportation from Park and Ride lots and downtown Chapel Hill, with passes costing $10 per person (kids 5 and under free).

Chapel Hill Parks and Recreation will adjust facility operating hours starting July 6 to align with community usage and ensure responsible use of taxpayer dollars. Additionally, the Chapel Hill Community Center is set to reopen June 1 after HVAC system replacement.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill has policies regarding advisory board and commission member attendance, leave of absence, and term limits. Members missing three consecutive meetings or over half in a year will be replaced. Leave of absence requires Council approval and cannot exceed five months. Members generally serve no more than two consecutive full terms (three for Board of Adjustment), with eligibility to serve again after a one-year absence.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill offers paid internship opportunities, including the Bill Thorpe Undergraduate Internship Program (up to $2500 compensation) and the Mayor's Internship program (up to $1000 per semester). These programs aim to provide students with real-world experience in local government, working with town leadership and departments, and contributing to the community.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This initiative details the scheduling and functions of various Chapel Hill town boards and commissions, including the Community Design Commission, Board of Adjustment, Historic District Commission, Criminal Justice Debt Program Advisory Committee, and Planning Commission, outlining their roles in guiding development, land use, and community character through meetings in 2026.

Chapel Hill's transit initiative covers fare-free bus services, real-time bus tracking, and transit advertising to offset operating costs. It also includes policies on public street usage, prioritizing safe vehicular traffic flow over unauthorized spontaneous events.

Chapel Hill offers fee waivers and pro-rata refunds for planning review fees for affordable housing units in development projects, contingent on an Affordable Housing Performance Agreement. Waivers also extend to public projects and non-profit service projects.

impacted_stakeholders: Criminal Justice Debt Program Advisory Committee
summary: Chapel Hill's Criminal Justice Debt Program, in partnership with the Police Crisis Unit, assists eligible indigent Orange County residents with post-conviction court fees, deferred prosecution costs, and NCDMV license restoration fees to support stability and community reintegration.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill annually establishes and publishes user fee policies, fee schedules, and facility use policies for various Town services, including business permits, planning, transit, building development, parks and recreation, public works, police, fire, parking, and arts and culture.
Chapel Hill Fire Rescue is investigating a house fire on Redbud Lane that resulted in one fatality. The investigation is ongoing to determine the fire's cause and the victim's identity.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: Chapel Hill provides resources for residential and commercial building permits and inspections, code enforcement, business services, active development project information, planning and zoning resources, and stormwater construction regulations.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill is soliciting statements of qualifications from firms for consulting services on the Bus Rapid Transit project and proposals from companies for janitorial services at multiple Parks and Recreation facilities.
Chapel Hill's athletics leagues depend on volunteer coaches, and the Town invites individuals passionate about youth sports to apply for coaching positions to ensure the leagues' success.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill's Town Council follows parliamentary procedures for deliberations, outlining rules for various motions (main, subsidiary, amend, substitute, reconsider, adjourn), voting, and appeals to ensure orderly meetings and fair decision-making.

Chapel Hill implements a Flood Damage Prevention Ordinance requiring structures in floodplains to be elevated at least two feet above base flood elevation to reduce damage and potentially lower flood insurance rates. The town provides information on flood risks, FEMA maps, and offers assistance with Letters of Map Change and elevation certificates. It also maintains a Hazard Mitigation Plan to reduce property damage from natural disasters and ensures eligibility for state and federal mitigation funds.

Chapel Hill enforces Resource Conservation Districts (RCDs) as an overlay zoning district to protect stream corridors, preserve water quality, minimize flood damage, and prevent erosion. RCD provisions limit structures and development within streamside buffer areas. The town provides a process for stream determination and RCD boundary delineation, with specific regulations for building activities within RCDs and FEMA regulatory floodplains, including requirements for permits and potential variances.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill maintains a commitment to financial transparency and accountability by providing public access to annual budgets, financial reports, quarterly updates, fee schedules, capital improvements program, and debt management information. These resources inform residents about public fund allocation and management.

Chapel Hill is engaged in several ongoing community initiatives and projects, including the Bus Rapid Transit upgrade to improve transportation, the Vision Zero Chapel Hill initiative for road safety and equitable mobility, and the Everywhere-to-Everywhere Greenways Feasibility Study to create a safer, more connected town. Other activities include adjusting Parks and Recreation facility hours, community food distribution, and celebrating civic events.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill has a uniform policy for accepting gifts and donations, distinguishing between those requiring Town Council approval (e.g., real estate, personal property with significant expenditure, restricted cash for non-current operating plans) and those the Town Manager can accept (e.g., unrestricted cash, cash for specified programs in current operating plans). The policy also outlines guidelines for donor acknowledgment labels.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill has established an application and appointment process for its advisory boards and commissions. The Town Clerk manages applications, advertises vacancies, and verifies eligibility. Boards and commissions review applicants and recommend candidates to the Town Council, which makes final appointments in open meetings, with provisions for filling mid-term vacancies and handling applications received after deadlines.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill conducts public hearings to gather input on various matters, including zoning changes, subdivision applications, and special use permits. Procedures are established for giving public notice, the format of hearings, and requirements for testimony under oath, especially for quasi-judicial matters like special use and subdivision hearings, where decisions are based solely on sworn evidence presented.

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: Chapel Hill provides guidance to residents on how to access property information through the Register of Deeds in Orange and Durham Counties. This includes instructions for searching property identification numbers (PINs) and addresses using county GIS mapping systems and public databases for property records.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill follows specific procedures for holding closed sessions as permitted by the Open Meetings Law. These sessions are limited to discussions on topics such as consulting with the Town's attorney, negotiating real property acquisition, or considering employee performance. While some actions can be taken in closed session, final actions are generally required in open session, and minutes are kept, though they may be withheld from public inspection under certain conditions.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Chapel Hill Council's practice for making appointments to boards and commissions involves voting by signed written ballot, as required by North Carolina Law. The Town Clerk manages the ballot process, tabulates votes, and announces results in open meetings. Ballots are available for public inspection, and procedures are in place for tie votes or insufficient votes, allowing for re-voting or delayed appointments.

impacted_stakeholders: property owners next to the treated section of river
summary: This topic covers Hillsborough's drinking water supply from the Eno River, including the Water Treatment Plant, the West Fork Eno Reservoir (Phase 1 operational in 2000, Phase 2 completed in 2021), emergency water connections with neighboring utilities, history of addressing water shortages, invasive plant (Hydrilla) treatment, water quality testing (over 150 substances), detailed water treatment process, annual chlorine disinfection, water system flushing, water line break responsibilities, and management of black/pink residue and stale water.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This topic outlines the Town of Hillsborough's social media policy, emphasizing that sites are moderated and subject to public records laws. It details acceptable and unacceptable content (e.g., related to town-sponsored activities, no profane language, no discrimination, no political campaigns, no solicitations). It also includes disclaimer statements regarding official communications, endorsements of ads, and the inability to answer questions or emergency requests via social media.

This topic describes Hillsborough's wastewater treatment and collection system. It highlights the release of treated wastewater into the Eno River, strict state requirements due to the Upper Neuse River Basin and Falls Lake Rules (Stage 1 met in 2014, Stage 2 expected 2036), and successful reduction of nitrogen and phosphorus discharge. It details the treatment process, water quality testing (over 220 substances), wastewater plant operations, pump station maintenance, responsibilities for sewer line blockages, preventative maintenance (hydraulic cleaning, smoke testing), and infrastructure protection programs (Fats, Oils, and Grease, No Wipes in the Pipes).

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances
summary: This topic provides guidance for developers, real estate agents, property seekers, engineers, and utility contractors regarding water and sewer availability, utility extensions, and connections in Hillsborough. It refers to the Code of Ordinances for utilities and sanitary sewer use, planning requirements, and fees for new connections and system development (charged per bedroom for residential permits). It also lists documents like developer/engineer checklists, fee schedules, and technical specifications.

Exceptions for emergency situations and specific police equipment are noted.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This topic provides a calendar of events and meetings for Hillsborough, including Bulk Items Collection Week, various board meetings (Board of Adjustment, Board of Commissioners, Historic District Commission, Parks and Recreation Board, Planning Board, Tree Board, Tourism Board, Tourism Development Authority), and town office closures (e.g., Memorial Day, Independence Day). It also mentions a Gold Park Pollinator Garden Workday and a public event "Together, We Are America".

This topic outlines the checklist and steps for starting, expanding, or relocating a business in Hillsborough. It covers determining zoning, selecting sites with economic development staff assistance, pre-development meetings (covering ADA compliance, building codes, health department requirements, parking, fire access), obtaining various permits (change of use, home occupation, new construction, signs), and coordinating with Orange County for inspections. It also details specific town services and permits related to business operation, including those for home occupations and the process for obtaining a business license for certain activities.

This topic describes Hillsborough's initiatives related to trees and invasive plant management. It highlights the weekly invasive species removal team led by the Hillsborough Tree Board, the town's participation in the Arbor Day Foundation's Tree City USA program since 2003 (requiring a tree board, care ordinance, and community forestry budget), and educational resources from Trees Are Good website. The Tree Board is responsible for trees/landscaping in parks, town property, and contributes to tree planting requirements in private development, and observes Arbor Day.

This topic provides water conservation tips for Hillsborough residents across various areas. In the kitchen and laundry, it advises reducing running water, rinsing vegetables in a pan, defrosting in microwave/refrigerator, using garbage disposal less, composting, filling sinks for handwashing dishes, using minimal detergent, and running full loads for dishwashers/washing machines, and choosing high-efficiency appliances. In the bathroom, it suggests turning off faucets, rinsing razors in filled sinks, shortening showers, taking shallow baths, installing low-flow fixtures, flushing only when necessary, and using a filled plastic bottle in toilet tanks. It also includes outside tips like reducing watering, avoiding vehicle washing at home, and delaying water-intensive projects, and dining out tips for bottled water during restrictions.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This topic details Hillsborough's policies regarding utility bill adjustments, particularly for water line leaks and malfunctioning water meters. Customers can request a leak adjustment within two months of the billing date, provided documentation of the leak or repair receipts. One leak adjustment is allowed per 12-month period, with the charge based on a six-month average. For malfunctioning meters, customers are charged based on a six-month average, and a meter flow test can be requested (with a fee if the meter passes). It also covers general billing information, payment methods, service disconnection, and online account management.

Hillsborough's policy outlines procedures for police officers regarding the humane destruction of badly injured domestic animals, including owner notification, supervisory approval, reporting protocols, and safety considerations for officers and the public.

The Hillsborough Police Department offers free home security checks and consultations to residents, providing advice on safety improvements and property monitoring to deter break-ins, especially when residents are away.

impacted_stakeholders: Orange County Planning and Inspections Department

Guidelines for Hillsborough residents to prevent water pipes from freezing and bursting, offering tips for insulating and heating pipes in unheated areas, sealing leaks, and steps to take if pipes are frozen or burst.

Hillsborough maintains a police canine unit with one dual-purpose patrol dog, Jett, acquired in 2018. The canine officers and their handlers undergo monthly training and annual certification by an independent trainer. The unit also performs demonstrations for schools and community groups.

Hillsborough's Town Cemetery requires burials to be performed by licensed funeral directors under town supervision, with specific notification requirements. All burial plots have been sold, with strict rules against reselling for profit or acquiring at an in-town rate for non-residents. The town issues Certificates of Burial Right and has regulations for grave markings and monuments, managed by the Public Works Division. Burial records are available online.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Hillsborough maintains a 'Community Contacts' program where volunteers share town information with neighbors and inform the Communications Division about topics needing more attention, aiming to keep the community informed.

Hillsborough is conducting a South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study to develop design concepts for a state-funded project to widen the road from Interstate 40 to the Eno River, aiming to enhance the town's character and transportation infrastructure.

Hillsborough is developing the Ridgewalk Greenway, a paved urban greenway for pedestrians and bicyclists, connecting downtown to a future train station and Cates Creek Park. This project, a transportation priority since 2021, will be implemented in sections and requires coordination with NCDOT and the NC Railroad Company, with developers of Collins Ridge contributing land for connectivity.

impacted_stakeholders: Internet Service Providers (Brightspeed, Google Fiber, Lumos, Spectrum)
summary: Hillsborough is seeing private utility companies like Brightspeed, Google Fiber, Lumos, and potentially Spectrum, install gigabit fiber-optic cables to enhance internet services. The town has master encroachment agreements with some providers and can impose conditions on work within public rights-of-way, but does not fund or perform the installations, which are private projects. Residents are advised to contact providers directly for service availability.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Town of Hillsborough is committed to not discriminating on the basis of disability, in compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act. It provides reasonable modifications to policies and procedures, including auxiliary aids for effective communication, welcomes service animals in town offices, and offers special solid waste collection for eligible residents with disabilities.

Hillsborough is undertaking a nearly $3.7 million renovation and expansion of the Highway 86 North Facility (890 N.C. 86 N.). This project will provide improved office, storage, and workspace for Fleet Maintenance Operations and the Safety and Risk Management Office, and will allow the Public Works Division to relocate its operations out of a floodplain into the renovated facility.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Hillsborough has content guidelines for social media, prohibiting profane, obscene, or sexual language/content, duplicate posts, and confidential or nonpublic information. Such content is subject to blocking or removal by the platform's filtering functions.

Hillsborough offers an Outdoor Seating Permit for businesses to serve customers on public sidewalks and spaces, subject to conditions like storefront placement, clear delineation of seating areas, and maintaining ADA-compliant and 6-foot clear pedestrian paths. Businesses selling beer or wine also require a separate business license.

The Lawndale Sewer Project in Hillsborough involves the repair and replacement of existing sewers in the Lawndale Basin. The scope includes cleaning, inspecting, performing small dig and repair projects on the main sewer line and service connections, installing or moving sewer cleanouts, and lining sewer pipes to prevent groundwater and rainwater infiltration. The project is estimated to cost $1.5 million, with funding from a low-interest loan and reserve funds, and is slated to begin in November 2025.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: This initiative outlines the procedures for starting and stopping water service in Hillsborough. To start service, customers must contact customer service at least three business days in advance, pay a $20 connection fee and a deposit, and provide a copy of a deed, lease, or property management agreement, along with a Social Security number and valid ID. Service can be initiated or disconnected via the town's online payment site.

Hillsborough's policy on reservations states that they are nonrefundable. No credit will be issued for bad weather if the park is open. However, cancellations made at least seven days before the reservation date may receive a credit for the amount paid, usable for one year. Reservations are approved at the discretion of authorized staff and will be canceled if payment is not received by the due date.

This policy outlines the circumstances under which a law enforcement officer may use deadly physical force. It is permitted to defend the officer or a third party from what the officer reasonably believes to be the use or imminent use of deadly physical force, or to effect an arrest or prevent escape from custody of a person attempting to escape by means of a deadly weapon or posing an imminent threat of death or serious physical injury to others.

The Hillsborough Police Department offers diverse training programs to enhance officer skills and safety. Internal training includes Brazilian jiu-jitsu to reduce the need for force and a virtual reality system for scenario-based responses. External training encompasses Crisis Intervention, the Law Enforcement Executive Program, Professional Certification, and West Point Leadership Training, all aimed at improving officer confidence, de-escalation techniques, and overall community safety.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments

primary_category: building permits, inspections, and local ordinances

Hillsborough is conducting a comprehensive parking study and developing a strategic parking management plan for its downtown and West Hillsborough areas. The initiative aims to assess current parking supply and demand, understand public perceptions, address future needs, improve parking accessibility and visibility, and develop strategies that align with broader community needs, evolving downtown demands, and the Comprehensive Sustainability Plan.

The City of Mebane's standards and specifications for street details and material specifications, made available for download, likely for construction and development projects.

Programs focused on introducing children and families to golf, such as the Family Team Series Program and the Mill Creek Jr. Golf Camp, emphasizing fun, learning through play, and family involvement in the game.

Warnings and advice for residents on how to protect themselves and their families from various scams, including those impersonating utility providers or family members in distress, and recommending verification steps like calling family directly or agreeing on a 'safe word'.

summary: The Orange County Department on Aging implements a five-year Master Aging Plan (MAP) to ensure comprehensive and coordinated delivery of community services and support, fostering lifelong engagement and well-being for the county's growing older adult population.

The Health Promotion and Education Services team in Orange County designs, implements, and evaluates health education programs, working with partners across the county. Specific programs include supporting the Board of Health, Healthy Carolinians of Orange County (improving health and wellness, creating health assessments), Quit Nicotine for Good! (group classes and counseling), and promoting Smoke-Free Orange County policies.

Orange County Animal Services (OCAS) runs a Foster Parent Hub to support fostering pets, aiming to reduce shelter stress and facilitate adoptions. The program encourages pet promotion through report cards and participation in adoption events like 'Foster Feature Saturdays'. It also provides resources for foster medical visits, including an emergency line and scheduled appointments, and detailed resources for fostering kittens, including neonatal training and weaning guidance. OCAS has an adoption policy for foster parents, emphasizing a first-come, first-served basis and staff decision-making.

The Orange County Soil & Water Conservation District provides technical, financial, and educational assistance to landowners and operators on erosion control and water quality. They offer cost-share programs, typically covering 75% of costs, to implement best management practices for agricultural, rural, and urban water quality and resource issues. Services include animal waste management, conservation planning, nutrient management, riparian restoration, sediment control, stream bank stabilization, voluntary agriculture and farmland preservation programs, closure of abandoned wells, community conservation assistance (CCAP), educational programs, forestry management, and wildlife habitat improvement.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments

Specific boards like Equalization and Review, Economic Development Advisory, Board of Adjustment, and Planning Board require disclosure statements. The county provides an online application process and manages appointments as vacancies occur, with applications remaining active for two years.

primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: Orange County departments utilize various social media platforms, including Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, and YouTube, to enhance outreach efforts and provide notifications to residents. primary_category: town governance, human resources, and board appointments
summary: The Orange County Government Academy is an eight-week program designed to educate residents aged 16 and older about the operations of Orange County government. It includes interactive learning sessions with UNC School of Government faculty and county staff from over 19 departments, as well as exclusive tours of county facilities like the 911 Call Center, Animal Services, and the Drakeford Library Complex. The program aims to foster community engagement and understanding of how residents can contribute to the community.

impacted_stakeholders: Orange County Department of Environment, Agriculture, Parks and Recreation
summary: Hikes with Hounds is a monthly enrichment program collaboration between Orange County Animal Services and the Department of Environment, Agriculture, Parks and Recreation. It allows adoptable dogs to leave the shelter for guided 3-mile hikes at Blackwood Farm Park with pre-registered volunteers. The program aims to provide mental and physical enrichment for dogs, offer meaningful volunteer experiences, increase community engagement and visibility for dogs needing homes, and support positive leash skills and socialization. Advance registration is required due to limited participation.

Orange County Animal Services highlights safety and environmental concerns associated with free-roaming cats. These include risks to cats from cars, animal cruelty, attacks by loose dogs and wild animals, and exposure to toxins like antifreeze and rodent poisons. Additionally, free-roaming cats pose a significant threat to bird populations and other small animals due to their strong prey drive, with millions of birds estimated to be killed annually by loose cats.

The Orange County Department of Social Services (DSS) offers various volunteer and donor opportunities to support local families and children. Additionally, the 'Friends of DSS' initiative helps with financial emergencies, material resources, and volunteer recruitment for staff support. The Heusner Fund assists foster children with college tuition and materials.

Orange County Animal Services (OCAS) implements spay/neuter programs to control pet overpopulation and reduce unwanted litters, aiming for a healthier community and fewer shelter animals. They offer no-cost spay/neuter vouchers for DSS clients and income-qualified residents, and provide information on low-cost options. A specific community cat spay/neuter program targets free-roaming, often feral or semi-feral, cats with the help of community cat caretakers for trapping, requiring a refundable deposit for traps. This approach aims to humanely manage free-roaming cat populations rather than just removing them.

Orange County Animal Services offers a comprehensive volunteer program to provide enrichment, comfort, and care for animals. Opportunities include dog walking, cat socialization, grooming, adoption support, event assistance, and community outreach. The program involves an application, virtual and in-person training, and mentored shifts, with specialized tracks for dog and cat volunteering, fostering, and 'Dog Adventures' programs.

The Orange County Department of Social Services provides preventive, supportive, and restorative services to protect vulnerable children, the at-risk elderly, persons with disabilities, and the economically disadvantaged. Orange County facilitates public donations through a General Recovery Fund, which allocates resources to various recovery areas including economic support for small businesses, and a GoFundMe portal dedicated to supporting Orange County Animal Services' lifesaving programs.

Orange County Animal Services operates a Kitten Foster Program to provide temporary homes and care for young kittens, particularly during 'kitten season,' to reduce shelter populations, promote socialization, and prevent disease. Orange County Animal Services (OCAS) partners with area rescue organizations for the placement of adoptable animals.
